The Girlboss Takeover Has a Data Problem

Table 1: Labor Force Participation Rate (%), Ages 20-29. Source: IPUMS CPS. Mike Konczal.
Sex Education 1990 2019 2024
Men College (BA+) 86.1 88.0 88.3
Women College (BA+) 83.4 84.7 85.1
Men Non-College 89.4 78.8 77.9
Women Non-College 72.1 69.5 68.9

The shift-share decomposition separates the aggregate change in women’s employment share into three components. Let \(s_{it}\) be women’s share of employment in industry \(i\) at time \(t\), and \(w_{it}\) be industry \(i\)’s share of total employment. The aggregate women’s share is \(\bar{s}_t = \sum_i w_{it} \, s_{it}\). We decompose its change from period 0 to period 1 as:

\[\Delta \bar{s} = \underbrace{\sum_i w_{i0} \, \Delta s_i}_{\textbf{Within}} + \underbrace{\sum_i \Delta w_i \cdot s_{i0}}_{\textbf{Between}} + \underbrace{\sum_i \Delta w_i \cdot \Delta s_i}_{\textbf{Interaction}}\]

where \(\Delta s_i = s_{i1} - s_{i0}\) and \(\Delta w_i = w_{i1} - w_{i0}\).

  • Within: Holding industry employment shares fixed at their 1999 values, how much did the gender composition change inside industries? A positive value means industries became more female on average.
  • Between: Holding each industry’s gender mix fixed at its 1999 level, how much did the reallocation of employment across industries change the aggregate? A positive value means the economy shifted toward industries that were already more female.
  • Interaction: A cross-term capturing industries that both grew and became more female (or shrank and became more male). Usually small.

This decomposition is exact: the three terms sum precisely to the total change, with no approximation or residual.
